name: Eurystheus
description: Eurystheus was the king of Tiryns and Mycenae in Greek mythology, best known for his role in assigning the Twelve Labors to Heracles. He was born prematurely, due to Hera's machinations, making Heracles, born later, subservient to him. Driven by fear and resentment of Heracles's divine parentage and immense strength, Eurystheus concocted a series of seemingly impossible tasks, hoping to bring about the hero's demise. These labors, while intended to be Heracles's undoing, instead became the foundation of his legendary status. Eurystheus himself was often depicted as a cowardly and insecure ruler, hiding in a bronze jar when Heracles returned from his labors. Despite his attempts to control Heracles, Eurystheus's actions ultimately contributed to the hero's apotheosis. After Heracles's death, Eurystheus continued to persecute his family, known as the Heracleidae, leading to further conflicts and ultimately his own downfall. He was eventually killed by Hyllus, Heracles's son, thus ending his reign of terror. Eurystheus's legacy is one of fear, envy, and ultimately, failure to subdue a true hero. His story serves as a reminder that even those in positions of power cannot escape the consequences of their actions, especially when driven by malice.
subtitle: King who tasked Heracles's Labors
achievements: Assigned the Twelve Labors to Heracles

keyMyths.title: The Twelve Labors
keyMyths.description: Eurystheus, driven by Hera's influence and his own fear, imposed twelve impossible tasks on Heracles. These labors became Heracles's defining trials and ultimately led to his immortal fame.

keyMyths.title: Hiding in a Jar
keyMyths.description: Fearful of Heracles's strength, Eurystheus was often depicted hiding in a bronze jar or pithos when the hero returned from his labors. This image highlights his cowardice and insecurity.
keyMyths.source: Various Greek vases and literary sources

keyMyths.title: Death at the Hands of Hyllus
keyMyths.description: After Heracles's death, Eurystheus continued to persecute his descendants, the Heracleidae, until Hyllus, Heracles's son, finally killed him in battle, avenging his father.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive summary of Greek mythology, providing details on Eurystheus's role in the Labors of Heracles.
sources.text: A comprehensive summary of Greek mythology, providing details on Eurystheus's role in the Labors of Heracles.

sources.author: Diodorus Siculus
sources.description: Offers historical and mythological accounts, including Eurystheus's reign and conflict with Heracles.
sources.text: Offers historical and mythological accounts, including Eurystheus's reign and conflict with Heracles.
